name: CCTV Risk Assessment Checklist new Jan 2026
description: Draft from Word file "CCTV_Risk_Assessment_Checklist new Jan 2026.docx". Review fields and adjust types before production.

fields.label: In this modern world, Closed-Circuit Television has become ubiquitous to our daily lives. They are an essential aspect of any security system, playing a role in almost every industry as a crime prevention tool or simply as a useful piece of surveillance equipment in order to ensure the smooth running of your business. If you are considering installing a CCTV system, it is important to conduct an adequate risk assessment before doing so, in order to ensure it is effectively and safely implemen…

Consult data protection regulations before installing your surveillance system. If the area is frequented by members of the public, you must then comply with the data protection act, which requires CCTV signs to be visible explaining that you are recording surveillance images and that this data may be stored indefinitely

Providers will also have to demonstrate that an individual's consent to the processing of their personal data is 'freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous', and in most cases implied consent will not be sufficient. The consent is found in the tenancy agreement.

Although in relation to the use of CCTV, it is still unclear to what extent you will need to seek to obtain explicit consent from individuals to record them via a CCTV system. As is already the case, you are required to make the presence of cameras very clear.

Have you made sure the CCTV system can be switched on or off if this is appropriate so that the recording of footage is not continuous? The system should also have the ability to stop capturing either footage and/or sound recordings, both of which should work independently of each other. Capturing both could be deemed excessive, and you would need to demonstrate clearly the reasons for recording both and what legitimate grounds you are relying on to justify this.

If you capture sound recordings, are they obtained when it is necessary and for this specific purpose? CCTV surveillance systems should not normally be used to record conversations between members of the public or members of staff as part of a working environment. Recording conversations is highly intrusive and unlikely to be justified.
Do all CCTV cameras serve a legitimate purpose?
Consult with staff, residents, and members of the public (visitors) that CCTV will be in operation and why
Consent forms issued and signed (if appropriate)
Are signs clearly visible that CCTV is in operation? Including where members of the public image may be recorded and stored
Data access requests available as per GDPR guidance
Are cameras located in well-lit areas so they will work at night? Consider a low light option?
Are cameras away from direct sources of heat to prevent overheating and possible fire?
If cameras are fitted outside, are they adequately protected from the elements?
A maintenance schedule in place to check the condition and functionality of the cameras
Video recording equipment is securely located, and password protected
A process for authorising staff to gain access to recordings
A process for removing authorised staff
If data is stored off-site, is there sufficient cyber security in place to prevent hacking?
A process for deleting recordings when they no longer serve a purpose
An emergency procedure in place if the system fails
Named person/s as the main contact point in the event of failure, breach or data access requests

description: Draft from Word file "Filey Care and Support LTD Citation Improvement Plan March 2026.docx". Review fields and adjust types before production.

fields.label: Filey Care and Support LTD Improvement Plan
Strengthen recording practices to ensure incident reports clearly describe what occurred, how staff intervened, and what actions were taken to keep people safe.
Review the use of restrictions or bans from activities to ensure they are proportionate, time-limited and regularly reviewed.
Introduce a structured post-incident review process to ensure that all safeguarding and other incidents are followed by a documented debrief with staff and, where appropriate, the person involved.
Review and update all care plans and risk assessments to ensure they are fully personalised, accurate and reflective of each person’s current needs.
All risk assessments must consistently and clearly describe triggers, early warning signs, proactive strategies and reactive interventions to reduce the likelihood of escalation.
Ensure all behaviour support plans are completed and regularly reviewed in partnership with Positive Behaviour Support (PBS) specialists where required.
Address inconsistencies in care documentation by ensuring that support levels are clearly defined and aligned across all plans, such as personal care, manual handling and repositioning requirements.

Review and update care plans and risk assessments to clearly evidence involvement of the person and family in care planning and review, documenting how staff support with communication and involvement in decision-making.

Ensure the dysphagia care plan accurately reflects the current diet texture level and remove incorrect references to puréed diets. Include clear guidance on recognising aspiration, choking risks and the escalation procedure staff must follow (Montague Gardens).
Where people supported show an increased risk of wandering, ensure the care plan clearly states that the monitoring device must be with the person when they leave their home.
Ensure all sections of the care plan are completed to provide optimum care outcomes, such as the activities section.
Skin integrity risk assessment tools to coincide with what the service uses.
Evidence the involvement of the people supported and their families in their care planning.
Ensure that people are actively involved in decisions about their daily lives, e.g., activities, routines and healthcare, to further strengthen person-centred risk management.
Update all care plans to include clear and specific guidance for staff, e.g., epilepsy seizure type, triggers, early warning signs, SUDEP awareness, etc.
Ensure staff sign records to indicate that they have read and understood that people’s plans are always up to date.
Ensure that there is evidence that information such as tenancy agreements and service user guides is explained to individuals, and evidence of this is kept.
Ensure the support needs detailed in support plans are reflected in daily records, e.g., daily records should reflect that a choice was being given.
Ensure records always guide people to be supported with dignity, e.g., describing people as ‘stubborn’.
Ensure keyworker sessions are happening as per their support plan.
Ensure risk assessments and support plans are reviewed regularly.
Ensure that care and support plans are in place, only where a need is identified.
Diabetes plans must include signs of hypo- and hyperglycaemia and actions staff must take in an emergency.
Ensure bowels and fluids are monitored correctly.
Ensure professional recommendations are fully reflected in care plans and regularly reviewed.

Review and update all staff recruitment records to ensure complete documentation, including verified employment history, references, interview notes, contracts, terms and conditions, and Right to Work checks.
Verify and file all references for staff, including overseas employment, and confirm DBS checks have been obtained, recorded, and are valid.
Review and reword the statement on the application form about the rehabilitation of offenders.
Staff interviews were not always conducted by two interviewers, and individuals' names were not always written in full.
Review and update the training matrix to ensure all completed training (e.g., Oliver McGowan Tier 1 & 2) is accurately recorded.
Identify and address gaps in training records, particularly in trauma-informed care and mental health, to reflect the needs of individuals supported.
Ensure records of staff training are always accessible.

Obtain and file all missing documentation, including Electrical Installation Condition Reports for Chase Road and Church Street, and ensure external fire risk assessments are accessible with actions clearly recorded as completed.
Review and update all fire emergency plans to include direct telephone contacts for the fire brigade and any additional site-specific risks.
Complete risk assessments for any ongoing maintenance work, including flooring installation at 52 Long Lane, considering obstacles, noise, and impact on people with mobility equipment.
Install signage for fire call points.
Ensure all overdue staff and volunteer risk assessments are reviewed and updated according to the stated schedule.
Servicing and inspection of fire panels at Church Street and 52 Long Lane should be completed immediately, with records filed and reviewed.
Standardise fire drill documentation across all sites with a single robust template.
Bring all health and safety documentation for Church Street up-to-date, including evidence of completed actions in the FRA, gas and fire panel servicing records, and EICR, etc.
Business continuity plans require expansion to include risks such as terrorism, fuel shortages, and extreme weather.
Ensure temperature monitoring records clearly show actions taken when storage temperatures fall outside recommended ranges.
Ensure MAR charts clearly record the exact dose administered, particularly where variable dosing is prescribed.
Seek clinical advice to clarify the acceptable administration window for medicines to ensure safe and consistent practice (Montague Gardens).
Medicine documentation is required to align with prescribing records, e.g., paracetamol and lorazepam not recorded/reflected in the MAR chart.
PRN (as required) protocols did not contain sufficient detail or clear guidance between different medicines.
Use clinical assessment tools consistently to guide care interventions and monitor outcomes.
Encourage participation in activities that support physical movement, mental well-being, and social engagement, ensuring records reflect the support provided.
Oral health assessment training had not been completed, and care plans did not reference the requirement for pre-medication before certain healthcare appointments.
Review all Mental Capacity Act assessments to ensure they are decision-specific and not combined into a single document covering multiple decision
Ensure consent forms are only signed by a person with a relevant legal framework such as POA where a person is assessed to lack capacity
Strengthen best interest decision records to clearly demonstrate alternative options considered and why they were not suitable.
Where an individual practices their faith ensure that they are supported to engage in the wider faith based communities.
Encourage a consistent “doing with” approach rather than completing tasks on behalf of individuals where they are able to participate.
Monitor staff practice through observations and spot checks to ensure individuals are actively involved in daily routines.

Review and update all service documents, including tenancy agreements, consent forms, and service guides, to ensure they are in accessible, user-friendly formats, including pictorial versions where required.

Develop and implement a structured forward-planning framework that ensures all individuals have documented discussions about future care, including DNACPR/ReSPECT, palliative care, and end-of-life preferences, with regular reviews involving the person, their family, and relevant professionals
Appoint a Freedom to Speak Up Guardian across all services and ensure staff are informed of their role, how to access them, and the process for raising concerns independently of line management.

name: Fridge & Freezer Food Temperature Monitoring  Record
description: Draft from Word file "Fridge & Freezer Food Temperature Monitoring  Record.docx". Review fields and adjust types before production.

fields.label: Top Tips – Fridge / Freezer Food Temperature Monitoring

The fridge temperature should be between +2°C and +8°C. The ideal temperature for a freezer is -18°C or lower. Take appropriate action as soon as possible if temperatures fall outside of these ranges and record the action taken on the temperature monitoring chart.
Record temperatures at least once a day at the same time each day. Ensure the thermometer is reset at each recording to capture accurate readings.
Follow the manufacturer’s instructions for servicing and maintenance of the fridge/freezer units.
The fridge and freezer should be used only for storing food items. Do not store non-food items or personal belongings inside.

When an external thermometer is in use (i.e. not built into the unit), it is good practice to replace batteries every 3 months and record when this has been completed.See separate battery change recording sheet.
Ensure the thermometer probe is placed correctly, not touching the back wall, food items, or blocking air circulation. The probe cable should not interfere with the door seal.
Ensure food items are not touching the back plate of the fridge, as this can cause freezing or spoilage due to excess moisture.
Do not overstock the fridge or freezer, as this prevents proper air circulation and can affect temperature consistency.
Rotate stock regularly (First In, First Out – FIFO) and check expiry/use-by dates. Use labelling (e.g. coloured stickers) to identify short-dated items.
Ensure the fridge and freezer are cleaned regularly and defrosted when required, including drip trays. Prevent water build-up and maintain hygiene standards. Keep a dated record of cleaning.
Ensure food is stored appropriately and safely, including raw and cooked food separation, to prevent cross-contamination.
If room temperature affects food storage (e.g. dry goods), ensure it is monitored and maintained within safe limits in line with food safety guidance.
MONTH 	 	 YEAR   	 	AUDITED BY (monthly sign off)
Action taken if temp out of recommended range

It is good practice to change batteries in external thermometers as per manufacturer’s instructions at least 3 monthly intervals or sooner if needed to ensure the batteries maintain optimum performance and do not lose power.
This will reduce the risk of the thermometer suddenly not working.
Highlight the months in which to perform a battery change, that works best for your home.

fields.label: Pregnant Worker / New or Expectant Mother Risk Assessment Guidance
The pregnant woman is entitled to a risk assessment once they have informed the employer in writing of their pregnancy
Risks may change as the pregnancy progresses. If the person has any concerns at any stage in the pregnancy about the baby’s health or safety, they should speak to their manager
The pregnant person is entitled to paid time off for antenatal appointments, including the time it takes to travel to and from the appointment or classes.
The person cannot be required to take annual leave or work the time back.

It is important that the pregnant worker is involved in the risk assessment process to ensure all the relevant facts and issues are covered. The risk assessment should then be reviewed on a regular basis, this may have to be increased as the pregnancy progresses.

If the risk assessment identifies hazards that cannot be eliminated or adequately reduced then the pregnant worker’s duties (e.g. temporarily reducing hours, changing duties or place of work etc.) should be adjusted appropriately to ensure they are.
If that cannot be achieved locally, the pregnant worker should be re-deployed for the duration of the pregnancy to a safer environment.
if this cannot be achieved the legislation requires the pregnant worker to be suspended from work on full pay for as long as is necessary to protect the health of herself or the baby’s health.

NB These actions are only necessary where, as a result of an assessment of risk, there is genuine concern.  If there is any doubt the manager should seek advice from HR and occupational health on what the risks are and whether they arise from work.
New Mothers returning to work - Risk Assessment Guidance

It is important to remember that the definition of ‘new or expectant mother’ means a worker who is pregnant, who has given birth within the previous six months, or who is breastfeeding. ‘Given birth’ is defined in the regulations as ‘delivered a living child or, after 24 weeks of pregnancy, a stillborn child’.

Any risk assessment undertaken for new mothers returning to work should be identifying any risks to the new mother and / or their children i.e. communicable diseases or hazards for new mothers who are breastfeeding.
The risk assessment process should continue for the duration the new mother breastfeeds for.
In practice this means constantly reviewing the risk assessment undertaken when the new mother returns to work for the duration of the time the new mother breastfeeds.
The guidance on ‘Pregnant Women and Nursing Mothers’ that can be found  http://www.hse.gov.uk/pubns/indg373.htm

Guidance Notes – How to Complete the Service Improvement Plan (SIP)
Purpose of the Service Improvement Plan
The Service Improvement Plan (SIP) is a live quality-improvement document used to:
Record identified shortfalls, risks, or areas for development
Set out clear, measurable actions to address those issues
Track progress and evidence improvements over time
Demonstrate good governance, oversight, and learning to regulators and commissioners
The SIP should be updated regularly and used alongside audits, incident reviews, safeguarding outcomes, complaints, and quality visits.
Specific – clearly describe what is wrong
Risk-focused – explain the potential impact on people using the service
Action-oriented – show what will be done to improve
Reviewed – progress must be updated until the issue is closed
Avoid vague statements such as “monitor staff” or “review practice” without explaining how, when, and by whom.
Record the date the issue was identified.
This may differ from the SIP creation date.
Use the same date format consistently throughout the document.
State where the issue was identified include: Service users name and room number, also for example:
What is missing, incorrect, or not followed
“Daily notes were not consistently completed for two service users over a 3-day period.”
Explain the potential or actual risk- Rate as (Low/Medium/High), such as:
Risk to service user safety or wellbeing
This shows risk awareness and proportional response.
Detail specific actions required to address the issue, including:
Any training, review, or corrective action
Updates to policies, care plans, risk assessments, or systems
Actions should be clear enough that another manager could follow them.
Name the person responsible for completing the action, for example:
Avoid generic terms such as “staff team”.
Interim review dates if the action is ongoing
This helps demonstrate timely governance and follow-up.
Record evidence (e.g. training completed, audit repeated)
Each section should only include issues relevant to that theme, for example:
If an issue overlaps sections, record it in the most relevant primary section and reference related actions elsewhere if needed.
After inspections, safeguarding outcomes, or serious incidents
The Registered Manager is responsible for:
The SIP is not a fault-finding document.
It is evidence of learning, accountability, and improvement

fields.label: Staff Competency Assessment for the Management of Medicines
To be completed annually, or sooner if a concern is identified.
Has the member of staff completed training on the safe handling of medicines?
Has the member of staff read the medication policy and signed to indicate that they have done so?
Does the member of staff know how to access the medication policy if they wish to check any information?
Did the member of staff check the person’s records to establish if support was to be given with medication for the person?
Did the member of staff check the documentation available to establish where the medication is kept for that person?
Did the member of staff make sure that everything was properly prepared before starting to administer the medication, e.g. prepare a drink for the person

Did the member of staff wash their hands before starting to administer any medication and follow appropriate hygiene measures whilst administering the medication? E.g. wear gloves when applying creams.
Before preparing or administering the medication did the member of staff obtain the person’s consent?

If consent was not obtained was this part of a documented protocol for this person, such as covert administration, and is the member of staff satisfied that the correct procedures have been followed in the best interests of the person?
Before selecting, preparing or administering any medication, did the member of staff read the MAR chart accurately?
Did the member of staff check whether a dose had already been administered/taken by the person or if the medication had been cancelled?
If any directions are unclear or illegible on the MAR did the member of staff take appropriate steps to clarify the directions?
Was the medication selected checked against the correct MAR chart, including checking the person’s name on the label and MAR?
If the directions on the MAR chart differed from those on the label, did the member of staff take the appropriate steps to satisfy themselves as to the correct dose to be given?
Was the correct medication and dose selected at the correct time? Was consideration given to timing in terms of food or other directions on the label?
Was the medication prepared according to the directions and information on the MAR chart or any accompanying protocol?
Did the member of staff use the appropriate measure for any doses of liquid medication? e.g. oral syringe, graduated measuring cup?
Did the member of staff check the records to see how the individual prefers to take their medication or demonstrate that they knew this information and administer the medication accordingly?

Did the member of staff offer information, support and reassurance throughout to the person, in a manner which encourages their co-operation, promotes dignity and is appropriate to their needs and concerns?
Was the medicine administered correctly and a glass of water offered where appropriate?
Did the member of staff visually witness the individual taking all their medication?
If the medication was left for the person to take later was this done following a documented agreed plan and was this recorded on the MAR chart correctly?
If the medication was not taken was the appropriate advice sought and documented?
If the medication was not taken was it dealt with as given in the medication policy?
Did the member of staff sign the MAR chart immediately after the medication was administered?
If the medication was not given was an appropriate code entered on the MAR chart?
Were the MAR charts kept in the file or returned to the file after the administration was complete.
Did the member of staff check that there was sufficient medication at the person’s home for at least one week?

If there are shortages in medication noted did the member of staff take appropriate action to ensure the stock was replaced? E.g. alert the person/representative that more medication was required using the agreed process for that person or initiate the reordering process as appropriate for the individual
Was all medication returned to the agreed place once the process had been completed and placed tidily?
Did the member of staff check the storage requirements for medicines and alert the person to any special requirements, if necessary? Eg cold storage
Ordering, Receipt and Disposal of Medication
Where staff are responsible, did the member of staff record any medication received into the home using the correct documentation?
Where appropriate, did staff put new supplies of medication in the agreed place in such a way that the older supplies would be used first?
Where staff are responsible, did the member of staff order medication following the agreement for that person after checking currently held stock?
Was any out of date medication or medication no longer required dealt with following the documented agreement with the person.
Is the member of staff aware of what action to take if a person wants to take ‘over the counter’ medication?
Is the member of staff aware of what to do if a person has a minor ailment? Eg refers to a pharmacist, GP as appropriate. Does not offer advice.
If staff are to administer a non prescribed medication did they check if this had been agreed with the pharmacist/GP and that this agreement was documented?
If a non prescribed medication was administered was this from the original container as purchased and was the dose the person wanted to take within the directions given on the packaging?
If a non prescribed medication was administered did the member of staff record this correctly on the MAR chart?
Does the member of staff know who to contact if they need advice on medication?
Did the member of staff refer the person to the patient information leaflet or appropriate health care professional if the individual wants advice on medication?
Can the member of staff describe the correct process for what to do if they make an error?
Can the member of staff describe the correct process for what to do if they discover an error made by another member of staff?
(Please record any discussions held with the member of staff)
Considering the information from the assessment the member of staff has been assessed as (Please delete as appropriate)
• Demonstrating competence at this assessment to administer medication unsupervised.
• Demonstrating competence at this assessment to administer medication unsupervised with the exceptions identified below
• Requiring further supervision or training to administer medication unsupervised at this time.
Name of the member of staff making the assessment.
Signature of the member of staff making the assessment.
Signature of the member of staff being assessed.

name: Staff Risk Assessment
description: Draft from Word file "Staff Risk Assessment .docx". Review fields and adjust types before production.

fields.label: Please tick  the area that require a staff risk assessment:
Lone working responsibilities – Staff may work alone or with fewer colleagues compared to residential settings.
Change in service user needs – Supporting individuals with different conditions such as learning disabilities, autism, mental health needs, or behaviours that challenge.
Manual handling differences – Changes in moving and handling tasks, availability of equipment, or reduced staff assistance.
Medication management responsibilities – Greater independence in administering or supporting medication.
Community-based support duties – Supporting individuals outside the home (shopping, appointments, activities), which introduces additional environmental and public safety risks.
Driving responsibilities – If the role requires transporting service users using personal or company vehicles.
Use of different systems or procedures – Adjusting to different care documentation systems, risk assessment formats, or reporting procedures.
Previous incidents or injuries – If the staff member has a history of workplace incidents that could be affected by the new role.
Stress or confidence concerns relating to role transition – Adjustment from a structured residential environment to a more independent supported living setting.
Working hours or shift pattern changes – Different shift patterns such as sleep-ins, waking nights, or longer lone-working shifts.

fields.label: Understands the purpose and use of prescribed topical and emollient creams.
Seeks and obtains appropriate consent before application.
Demonstrates correct hand hygiene before and after application. Uses gloves appropriately.
Checks the MAR chart/care plan, correct cream, expiry date, and correct service user.
Applies the cream/emollient correctly: clean area, apply thin layer, and avoid rubbing vigorously.
Understands fire risks associated with emollients building up on clothes, bedding, and bandages.
Explains procedure to the service user in a respectful and age/ability-appropriate manner.
Checks the area before and after application for irritation, rash, broken skin, or adverse reaction.
Accurately records time, date, area treated, and any observations on the MAR chart or care notes.
Knows how and when to report concerns (e.g., skin reactions, refusal, changes in condition).
Ensures creams are stored safely, securely, and in accordance with service policy.
Is the support worker competent to apply topical/emollient creams independently?
Emollients can soak into fabrics and remain flammable even after washing.
Ensure regular washing of clothes and bedding to reduce build-up.
Avoid smoking or open flames around service users using emollient creams.
Always follow guidance in the care plan and MAR chart.
